cases.passage: The Emergency imposed by Indira Gandhi in 1975 led to a grave crisis of the constitutional order. The 21st Amendment to the Constitution, which gave the President sweeping powers to make laws, bypassing the Parliament, was seen as an attempt to undermine the democratic institutions. The press was muzzled, dissent was suppressed, and opposition was banned. The Congress government used the Emergency to silence its critics and consolidate its power. The Supreme Court, in the famous case of Keshavananda Bharti vs. State of Kerala, upheld the validity of the 21st Amendment, but also laid down the 'Basic Structure Doctrine', which limited the power of the Parliament to amend certain fundamental features of the Constitution.

questions.q: Why did the 21st Amendment to the Constitution raise concerns about the crisis of the constitutional order?
opts: (A) It gave the President limited powers to make laws.
(B) It allowed the Parliament to bypass the President's approval.
(C) It gave the President sweeping powers to make laws, bypassing the Parliament.
(D) It limited the power of the Supreme Court to review laws made by the President.
questions.explanation: The 21st Amendment gave the President sweeping powers to make laws, bypassing the Parliament, which raised concerns about the crisis of the constitutional order.

questions.q: What was the consequence of the Supreme Court's decision in the case of Keshavananda Bharti vs. State of Kerala?
opts: (A) It led to the abolition of the Emergency.
(B) It limited the power of the Parliament to amend the Constitution.
(C) It upheld the validity of the 21st Amendment, but also laid down the 'Basic Structure Doctrine'.
(D) It led to the resignation of Indira Gandhi.
questions.explanation: The Supreme Court's decision in the case of Keshavananda Bharti vs. State of Kerala upheld the validity of the 21st Amendment, but also laid down the 'Basic Structure Doctrine', which limited the power of the Parliament to amend certain fundamental features of the Constitution.

questions.q: What was the impact of the Emergency on the press and dissent in India?
opts: (A) The press was given more freedom to criticize the government.
(B) Dissent was encouraged and freedom of speech was upheld.
(C) The press was muzzled, dissent was suppressed, and opposition was banned.
(D) The government imposed strict censorship on the press, but allowed some dissent.
questions.explanation: The Emergency led to the muzzling of the press, suppression of dissent, and banning of opposition in India.

questions.q: What was the significance of the 'Basic Structure Doctrine' laid down by the Supreme Court in the case of Keshavananda Bharti vs. State of Kerala?
opts: (A) It limited the power of the Parliament to amend the Constitution.
(B) It gave the President unlimited powers to make laws.
(C) It allowed the Parliament to bypass the President's approval.
(D) It had no significant impact on the Constitution of India.
questions.explanation: The 'Basic Structure Doctrine' laid down by the Supreme Court in the case of Keshavananda Bharti vs. State of Kerala limited the power of the Parliament to amend certain fundamental features of the Constitution.

questions.q: Who was the main beneficiary of the Emergency imposed by Indira Gandhi in 1975?
opts: (A) The opposition parties.
(B) The press and dissenting voices.
(C) The Congress government and Indira Gandhi.
(D) The President of India.
questions.explanation: The Congress government and Indira Gandhi were the main beneficiaries of the Emergency imposed in 1975.

cases.passage: The Ayodhya dispute, which led to the demolition of the Babri Masjid in 1992, was a major crisis of the constitutional order in India. The dispute was rooted in the communal tensions between Hindus and Muslims, which had been simmering for decades. The demolition of the mosque was seen as a violation of the rule of law and an attack on the secular principles of the Constitution. The government's inability to prevent the demolition and its subsequent failure to deliver justice to the victims of the incident raised concerns about the crisis of the constitutional order.

questions.q: What was the root cause of the Ayodhya dispute?
opts: (A) The dispute over the ownership of the land.
(B) The communal tensions between Hindus and Muslims.
(C) The economic interests of the local community.
(D) The political ambitions of the ruling party.
questions.explanation: The Ayodhya dispute was rooted in the communal tensions between Hindus and Muslims, which had been simmering for decades.

questions.q: What was the consequence of the demolition of the Babri Masjid in 1992?
opts: (A) It led to a major communal riot in Ayodhya.
(B) It led to the strengthening of the secular principles of the Constitution.
(C) It was seen as a violation of the rule of law and an attack on the secular principles of the Constitution.
(D) It led to the resignation of the Prime Minister.
questions.explanation: The demolition of the Babri Masjid in 1992 was seen as a violation of the rule of law and an attack on the secular principles of the Constitution.

questions.q: What was the role of the government in preventing the demolition of the Babri Masjid?
opts: (A) The government deployed the army to prevent the demolition.
(B) The government failed to take adequate measures to prevent the demolition.
(C) The government was not involved in the demolition of the Babri Masjid.
(D) The government was responsible for the demolition of the Babri Masjid.
questions.explanation: The government failed to take adequate measures to prevent the demolition of the Babri Masjid.

questions.q: What was the impact of the Ayodhya dispute on the rule of law in India?
opts: (A) It strengthened the rule of law in India.
(B) It had no impact on the rule of law in India.
(C) It weakened the rule of law in India.
(D) It led to the abolition of the rule of law in India.
questions.explanation: The Ayodhya dispute weakened the rule of law in India.

questions.q: What was the significance of the Ayodhya dispute in the context of the crisis of the constitutional order?
opts: (A) It was a minor incident that had no impact on the Constitution.
(B) It was a major crisis of the constitutional order that threatened the secular principles of the Constitution.
(C) It was a constitutional crisis that led to the resignation of the Prime Minister.
(D) It was a judicial crisis that led to the impeachment of the Chief Justice.
questions.explanation: The Ayodhya dispute was a major crisis of the constitutional order that threatened the secular principles of the Constitution.
